quote: Before devoting the many months or even years of developing a scripted series, I urge you to first ask yourself three basic questions: Why this idea? Why you as its author/creator/producer? Why now?

Why you as its author/creator/producer? Why are you uniquely qualified and compelled to write this pilot and/or to steer this series? What about the idea appeals to you? How is it personal to you – even if it's not autobiographical?

What you don't know can hurt you in a pilot script and/or pitch and/or pitch document. Of course, no one is expecting 100% historical or procedural accuracy. Verisimilitude, or the 'ring of truth,' in the service of drama is perfectly fine, too.
Bottom line: make a case as to why you're the ideal writer for this project. Get personal. A pitch has a performative aspect, a paradox for writers who often prefer solitude.

Why now? Timing is everything. Look at the world around you, near and far. What outrages you? What stories are not being told or told well? How can your project fill a void of stories set in a particular world or about a unique group of people?

Don't attempt to chase current programming mandates at each studio or network. By the time you've finished and polished your pilot script and/or pitch document, those mandates will have already changed. Instead, dig deep and get personal.
Specificity and personalization are how to help ensure that your idea is not common, generic, or derivative. Each of us is unique. Each pilot and series pitch ought to be unique and relatable, too.
The more specific you make something, the more universal it becomes.
To me, proof-of-concept is best achieved with an exceptionally well-written, polished pilot script, accompanied by a pitch document that forecasts what's going to happen in episode 2 and beyond.

Every script you write has the potential to be a potent calling card. A verbal pitch, even with a knockout pitch deck and/or look book, won't convey your writing talent when it comes to the nuances of character, dialogue, tone, subtext, world building, etc.

A pilot episode used to be intended as the prototype for future episodes in terms of format, tone, style, POV, pacing, etc. Today's TV landscape defies formula, and subsequent episodes can be innovative and/or anomalous to avoid a formulaic, predictable pattern.
There is no longer an absolute industry standard regarding the requisite number of episodes per season.
If you're conjuring up a new episodic series, I advise creators to use a benchmark of ten episodes per season that may expand or contract, depending on the buyer.
The end of your pilot is the beginning of your series.

A strong pilot script will often end with the writer leaving the reader with myriad questions and personal challenges that each main character will face, conveying that we absolutely must keep reading more episodes to get answers over the course of the season(s).

When we write about or talk about where a series is going, we're talking about character development and evolution (aka 'character arcs'), not plot points. I prefer the term 'character journeys.' Plot must always be married to character; otherwise, we're just witnessing random strangers moving arbitrarily through time and space.
Plot is all about who, what, when, where, and how. Story is character-driven and encompasses 'the why?'

To be captivated by a pilot script, we need to feel empathy for at least one of the principal characters. We need to worry about them. We need to root for them – even if their goals are illicit. We need to care.

If you can achieve empathy in your pilot script, you're evoking an emotional response in your audience and potential buyer. Empathy is what makes a script resonate. I'd like to posit that empathy is more important than contrived plotting and flashy visual effects.
If we don't connect to characters with actual strengths and weaknesses (aka vulnerabilities), then no buyer or audience will be interested.
Hook: Articulated as 'What if...?' For example, Ted Lasso: What if an American football coach was hired to coach a 'football' (aka soccer) team in England?

Logline: Must contain the word 'but.' Examples: Ted Lasso: A folksy American football coach from Wichita, Kansas, is recruited to coach a football (aka soccer) team in England, but he has zero experience as a soccer coach.
Note that it's harder to sell a limited series, as networks and studios still prefer a sustainable franchise. If the show is a hit, naturally they're going to want more seasons.

Story Engines: What will drive the series from episode to episode? Characters' trials and tribulations fuel the story engines. How a problem is proactively solved usually involves a 'micro mission' – which 'fuels' each story engine.

Teaser/Cold Open: Everyone loves to hear a great story told by a master storyteller, so start strong. If you're writing a comedy series, start off 'cold' with a funny, clever, and/or ironic cold opening. If writing a drama series, start off 'hot' with a provocative teaser.

Theme(s) to Explore: There's the story, and then there's what the story is really about on a universally relatable and human level: that's theme. House of Cards isn't just a show about politics – it's about power. Schitt's Creek isn't solely about a fish-out-of-water family – it's about loss. The Queen's Gambit is about abandonment, loneliness, and the price of genius.

Central Question: Concerns the future. Where is the show going? What'll happen to these characters that will challenge them and pull them out of their comfort zones? What big problem must they solve/overcome over the course of the season and series?
Central Mystery: Concerns the past. What happened before? How and why are the characters behaving in unique, specific ways? The answers are in their backstory. Is it an open or closed mystery?

Dramatic tension emerges from the three things in life none of us can control: (1) the environment (weather, acts of God, time, mortality, and – increasingly – technology); (2) our own emotions; and (3) other people. Throw them all at your characters.
Great TV is about cool people doing cool shit.

The series bible is a valuable tool to prove that your series 'has legs' and can walk the distance versus a more finite movie experience. A common critique of many good ideas can be: 'Great idea, but it feels more like a movie than a TV series.'

Structure, Pacing and Relationship to Time: Time is the container of your series. How much time does season 1 encompass in the characters' lives? Does it happen during one 24-hour day? Over several weeks or months? Over the course of several years?
Narrative Devices: With V.O., less is more. Use sparingly, artfully, ironically, and/or humorously – or else it's a narrative crutch.
Audiences are more compelled by what they don't know than by what they do. That's why we keep watching.

Metaphors and Visual Motifs: Mrs. Maisel's standup comedy career is a metaphor for feminism in the late 1950s and 1960s. Motifs can take many forms and can add a potent extra layer to the world when used subtly and effectively.

Aranya: The number one question I get all the time. I have salespeople or distributors or whoever call me and say, 'What are you looking for?' And I always disappoint them because I can't say how 'We're looking for this kind of thing, or exactly this or that.' I don't run my business that way. I'm more spiritual about it, a little more personal about it in the sense that I fall in love with stories.

Aranya: Most poorly done pitch documents come my way from the international market. I will tell you they often don't really even contain a story. There's a lot of pictures and the deck and stuff. There's this world and that world... And I'm like, 'Yeah, but what's the show?'

Aranya: I should make a distinction that I share in my classes that I think is really important. What I often see in pitches outside of the US is that they're still selling worlds. They're not selling a pitch. This is a mistake. They've collapsed the distinctions. They think simply describing the world of the show is enough. It's not.

Aranya: The American writer, when they come in to a pitch, they've done a lot of homework. They're ready to go. They've built the story. They've thought about the season. They've thoroughly thought about the pilot.

Aranya: I'm not a studio executive. I am not a buyer. I'm not working for Netflix. I'm a producer who understands storytelling. As a producer, the only thing I need to see is whether I can help make something out of it. Don't worry about the condition of it right now. If it's interesting, I will help find a way to move it from that condition into a place where it's viable for the marketplace.

Aranya: I share that view, but I have a broader view on it in a sense. And this is why I'm so gung-ho about pitch documents. Not to say I'm not gung-ho about scripts. Of course, I am. But in the world of people offering me shows and me pitching shows as a producer on American shows, I only need a pitch document.

Aranya: The marketplace is actually saturated with spec scripts. There's a traffic jam. So, when people say, 'Oh, it's better to have a completed script,' and I quote my mentor from NYU who had a great saying, 'Remember, a script is also 60 pages of reasons to say no.'

Aranya: Maybe the dreamy pitch that I gave them was much more powerful than reading anything else, and my hunch was that the pitch was enticement enough, and then let their imaginations do the rest. And it paid off.

Aranya: More times than not, we do not need a script. A script can only get in the way. I went out with two packages in my life that were developed in-house and both of them did not sell. And those were good scripts. So, there's no one way to do it.
Aranya: I think it's discovering where your story is most powerful, homing in on that power, and knowing how to own it and deliver it to people. For me, that's how a great pitch works.

Aranya: With serialized drama, the story of the characters is story, and it keeps going. So, for me, it's hard to pitch a teaser for a serialized drama, then stop and just talk about the characters and then start again. To me, it ruins the flow of the pitch because I pitch my characters while I'm pitching the story. I'm not separating the two. There they are woven together.

Aranya: In pitches, I'm trying to define the power in each story. With Your Honor, it was clear to me that when the father, the respectable judge decides he has to cover up for his son Adam's crime, you can stop there. The pitch is done.

Aranya: Personally, I don't. I grew up in a time that people told us that when you submit a feature script, don't put it in a fancy cover because that's covering up for something. And so that informed the way I pitch. And my reaction to reading all these decks with all the imagery and no story is that I'm going to do mine with 98 percent text.

Creators should be very cognizant of making their shows internationally appealing. Whether you're making it for a streamer that wants to get viewership in a variety of territories or you're in a traditional business where your studio needs to go out and sell territory by territory, you're very much relying on the dynamics of the international marketplace.
Intellectual property (I.P.) is source material that is often (typically) not created by the screenwriter. In today's TV landscape, the majority of shows are based on I.P.

I would definitely not waste your time adapting a work you do not own or control. To avoid getting tangled up in legalities and securing rights, I instead encourage emerging writers to (1) create their own original series unencumbered by proprietary rights; (2) create their own I.P. first and then adapt it; (3) adapt works in 'the public domain.'
In general, ideas cannot be copyrighted; only the hyper-specific expression or execution of said idea can be copyright protected.

Structure cannot be protected by copyright law. That's right – the screenwriters of Rashomon, Memento, Showtime's The Affair, Groundhog Day, Netflix's Russian Doll, and Hulu's Palm Springs are all free and clear as far as violation of copyright laws are concerned for the use of their 'borrowed' structural elements.

Streaming, on-demand television is the ideal medium to tell lengthy sagas that may span decades and include a cast of thousands. Look no further than these book-to-TV-screen juggernauts: Game of Thrones, My Brilliant Friend, and Bridgerton. Russian literature is ideal for the ongoing TV series format.

There is no formula for adaptation. The era of linear, chronologically unspooled narratives is mostly over. Episodes no longer need to offer a predictive pattern, and they may flash back or flash forward as the story unfolds, adding layers of empathy and understanding. The best approach to any adaptation is bespoke.

Theme: You might want to choose an overarching theme to examine over the course of a season, with a new theme emerging in the season-to-season pivot. What new territory will be explored in each season to avoid repetition?
End-of-season cliffhangers: In an ongoing series, each season will ideally end with a big revelation, preceding an even greater challenge in the season to come.

Character journeys (aka arcs): This will encompass structure, as characters tend to evolve over time. In each subsequent season, viewers anticipate new emotional territory to mine, new challenges, and new ways for us to see how beloved characters will perform under pressure.
Honoring the spirit and tone of the book (or other source material): You can't please everybody, but honoring the spirit and tone of the source material is a good place to start.

Once you shift from page to screen, the moving image supersedes the written word. At some point in the adaptation process, dramatic license takes precedence over remaining completely loyal to the original text as if it were sacred.

Finding the (emotional) core: Even with multiple seasons to tell a sprawling, novelistic story, you'll probably want to avoid a kaleidoscopic or scattershot approach. Today's audiences are quite sophisticated and adept at connecting with and tracking many characters. But don't test your viewers' goodwill with too many characters and too much plot. Without emotion, a series will invariably feel empty.

It's not solely who does what when, but who knows what when and the corresponding emotional fallout and ripple effects of the plot. Even characters who are cut off from their emotions can evoke our empathy. Repressed feelings are still feelings and tend to lead to catharsis (from Aristotle), the lifeblood of any story worth telling.

Externalizing inner psychology: Novels have the great benefit of omniscience and/or POV – and are capable of interior monologue to clue in the audience to a character's thoughts, hopes, dreams, worries, and fears. In a TV series adaptation, a singular POV might shift to multiple points of view, or a single, mysterious narrator.

As the great mystery author turned TV writer/producer Walter Mosley once said: 'Plot Is Revelation.' Gradually unraveling plot is akin to peeling an onion, and episodic TV allows that better than any other visual medium.
Cinematic Television is not an oxymoron: When approaching an adaptation, ask yourself if telling the story visually will enhance or overshadow the tone and intent of the source material.

Costanzo: I write to the subject of each episode. I go from the book and I write a kind of subject summary: four pages on what the episode is talking about. I just make a kind of map for each episode. And then I give it to the writer and the writer works from those pages. So it's not a real writing room. It's more like me giving them the structure of each episode and then they write it.

Costanzo: I'm not a showrunner. I see myself as a writer-director. I couldn't manage a group of writers giving the scripts to different directors. So what I did from the beginning was to act like I was writing a film, with the same kind of freedom.
Costanzo: Every episode is comprised of three acts, plus prologue and epilogue. And every scene is three acts, plus prologue and epilogue.

Costanzo: It was something I needed to go from one big scene to another big scene. If you look at it, you'll see that each episode of My Brilliant Friend is basically 10–15 scenes, at most, when usually a TV show script is a scene every page, every page and a half. But I like to make a big scene.

Costanzo: For me, the best compliment you can give to a work is that it is alive. That it is real. The heart is beating. This is happening for real. You are looking for an accident all the time. You look for something that makes whatever you had planned or written feel almost obsolete because whatever is happening in front of you is much deeper. This is brilliant because it was unexpected. That is the way I work on a film.

Costanzo: I found that I followed the book because the linearity of the book is really working, in my opinion; it's like she's writing a diary. It's like an interior monologue. And how will we translate that same kind of urgency that Elena Ferrante had in the book? That was the main question I was asking myself.

Hawley: I think so. The challenge always in telling the long-form story is that each episode has to have a beginning, middle, and end that has to fit into a larger beginning, middle, and end of the season, so there are structural challenges all the way through.
Hawley: I realized early on that if I did the tone of the show as the actual tone of the movie, people would think I got it wrong.

Hawley: Also, the difference between a ten-hour story and a two-hour story is we need to keep the tension going for ten hours. You need to feel there's a crime in hour one, and then the rest of it is all just solving that crime. You need a sense of danger to hang over the show. We started calling it 'No Country for Old Fargo.' It needs to have that kind of feeling to it, so that it continues to pull you forward.

Hawley: There's something very romantic about decency to me, and this idea of basically decent people who are probably in over their heads, and that's what heroism is. We've fallen into the Marvel universe where all of our heroes are 'super' now, or say between 1995 and 2005, our heroes became these tortured demon hunters.

Hawley: I'm an ensemble storyteller, and sometimes I feel like in my relationships with the network or other studios that I work with, you have to constantly teach them that it's OK to be an ensemble storyteller – that if you get this piece of the puzzle here and this piece of the puzzle there, it's all going to end up with the puzzle being solved at the end.

Hawley: Some of it is that we know violence is coming in Fargo. And I never want violence to be entertainment. You talked about the comedy of Steve Buscemi... It's like the shock of it, that moment where Steve Buscemi is like, 'Whoa, Daddy.' We see him go into shock.

Hawley: You want the audience to have that feeling. I don't want them to cheer for it. But in order for that to happen, you have to invest in the characters. You can't ever have a moment where there's going to be violence where you don't care about the person who's about to be killed.

Hawley: You also set up that anything can happen at any time, which is so different from what television used to be, where things just followed a predictable pattern. Anyone can die in any way in season 1.

Hawley: Closing the loop of the original crime allowed us to jump ahead almost a year, and now Molly's pregnant, then you go, 'Oh, wait, it is the movie.' It creates that echo, that rhyme, with the movie in a way, where now you're setting something up so that the audience, in the last hour, can presume that they know what's going to happen next... And then when that doesn't happen; you're watching something new and remembering something old at the same time. I think there's a tension to that that's really interesting.

Hawley: Each season has started with a moment. Season 1 was two men who meet in an emergency room, and one of them is a very civilized man and the other is the opposite... Season 2 was a woman drives home with a guy sticking out of the windshield of her car... Season 3 was twin brothers whose dad dies and leaves one of them a car and the other a stamp collection.

Hawley: What struck me first in the genesis of the story, which was not me sitting in a room for weeks at a time thinking about the story – it was an idea here, and then a couple of weeks later, a different idea that seemed like it might be tied to that first idea.
Hawley: I like that process of: you solve the problem, create a problem, solve the problem, and then one day, you'll be out of problems and the show will be over.

Hawley: When you have an ensemble, there's usually gravitational pull toward what happened to, say, Peggy Blumquist, and you can layer it into the overall story so that you never leave the main action. There's also tension when leaving the main action. There's tension to the way that episode ends and then having to wait a week to see what's happening with Loy and Josto and those characters.

Hawley: I like those episodes. They're usually my favorite, and I tend to have them, whether it's Ed and Peggy in the cabin as a stand-alone, or Gloria Burgle going to Los Angeles as a stand-alone. When you have an ensemble, there's usually gravitational pull... There's also tension when leaving the main action.

Hawley: You never want the audience to be so far ahead of you that they feel like it's a letdown when it finally happens. In the black-and-white episode where they think, 'Oh, wait, they're not going to end this with a tornado, are they, and go to color?' You never want to make it easy on the audience.

Hawley: When you're trying to tell a story that feels like a true story, you go, 'Well, that's just what happened.' I know it doesn't make dramatic sense because it's not white hat versus black hat in the town square, but it started that way and then the tornado came.

Gould: Generally, everything grows out of problem-solving. And most of the time the problem we're struggling to solve is, 'what's going on with this person?' What are they thinking? What are they feeling? What do they want to do next? What's going on under the surface? The better we understand the people, the better we understand the answer to the key question: what's next?

Gould: We frequently have ideas and images that stay with us but don't yet fit into our story. They're like these beautiful little puzzle pieces that don't yet have a place to land. Some of the best 'aha' moments in the writers' room come when we see the opportunity to use these puzzle pieces – when suddenly there's the perfect moment in our story for that scene, situation, or image we've been waiting for.

Gould: I'm always looking for physical situations that express what's going on with our characters. Bob is a brilliantly physical actor. The truth is that, as a performer, Bob thrives when his characters are suffering.

Gould: One of the fun things about working on both Breaking Bad and Better Call Saul is that we have very few structural obligations. Beyond act breaks and running time, we can experiment with the format as much as we like. For one thing, that means that our lead actors don't have to appear in every single episode.

Gould: On both Breaking Bad and Better Call Saul, we like to think of the whole show as one story. And if we're cutting between different characters who are up to different things, it's because those things mean something when juxtaposed together, and sooner or later their actions will all collide.

Gould: Sometimes when people are deprived of information, they fill the void with their own fears and doubts. That kind of communication is part of running a show that you don't hear about as much, but it's crucial.

Gould: The trick, if there is a trick, is to constantly look back at what we've already seen, to examine what's already been established and search for the little details that might have more meaning than we knew. Like the Stevia, sometimes these small details can turn out to be crucial. You know what they say, 'from tiny acorns do great oaks grow.'

Gould: We never toy with the audience. Sometimes it's fun when a cigar is just a cigar, when the clues lead exactly where you'd expect them to. For example, Saul Goodman saying, 'If I'm lucky, a month from now, best case scenario, I'm managing a Cinnabon in Omaha.' And then we see it happen.

Gould: I think that often what people are looking for in their entertainment is surprise – but that's not the most important thing. Sometimes in reaching for surprise, I think you can leave a lot of emotion and drama behind. I always think about Truffaut's interviews with Hitchcock. And Hitchcock argues for, as much as possible, choosing suspense over surprise.

Gould: I'd rather a viewer ask, 'How did this happen?' instead of 'Oh God, this character turns out not to be who I thought he was because the filmmakers fooled me.' The jolt that you get from realizing you've been fooled is over in a moment; the worry about your character's fate can be sustained much, much longer.
Gould: Continuous time helps build story momentum. It changes the rhythm of the storytelling and often makes for longer, more intricate scenes.

Gould: Something I learned from Vince on Breaking Bad is to be wary of time cuts. When we were breaking stories, it felt as if each time cut cost him 500 bucks out of his wallet. In other words, he's very careful with them. It's a stylistic choice.

Gould: It's always a struggle. I never assume that just because something worked in the past, it will work again. Every day I work on the show, I'm neurotic and worried and wracked with doubt. Like it or not, that seems to be my process.

Gould: Act breaks can be a useful organizing principle. I think it depends on the show. But, in terms of construction, I like to think Breaking Bad and Better Call Saul are fundamentally old-fashioned Drama 101 storytelling. What happens next? How did that happen? How did this come about?

Gould: Part of the art of telling an episodic story is not to resolve everything at the end of an episode. You want to have open business. It's actually really helpful in the writers' room to say, 'Okay, well, we know this scene doesn't fit into this episode or it feels anticlimactic, so that's going to kick us off on the next episode.'

Gould: I'm allergic to the kind of flashback which tells you, as the saying goes, 'the child is the father of the man.' I don't want to just see a miniature version of our characters. It can be fun; it can be cute, but in my book it's a mistake. If we go to the trouble of going back in time only to find this person was exactly the same in the past, you've taken away their agency.
Gould: If the characters don't want to do what we think they should do, we've got to change directions. That's rule number one. But I think – I think – we have answers for the big questions.

Gould: One of my favorite things about working in series television is that we get to watch the show as it progresses and make decisions based on what's actually been shot, instead of what we thought would be shot. A major part of that comes down to observing the performers and seeing what works.

Gould: Something I keep saying is 'you have to earn your pleasures.' These crossovers have to be earned. If we're going to bring back a beloved character who we've spent so much time with, they better be there for a reason; their appearance should somehow illuminate the story we're telling.
Gould: It's very much a matter of feeling our way through it rather than having a master plan.
Great television series provide us with a glimpse into an unknown world, or to a world we think we know – until we see it from an insider's perspective.

The 'arena' of your series is its setting, but it also encompasses time period, geography, weather, local customs, vernacular, style, traffic, values, social mores, and cultural, political and religious influences. In other words, all TV series are microcosms of the world at large.

Keep it real. I cannot encourage you enough to research, dig deeper, and mine the specifics of your intended arena. Not only will it provide you with context for character quirks and possible story ideas, but it will also make you an authority when it's time to pitch.
Find a baseline of 'grounded' reality, even in a fantasy world, by asking yourself: What if this really happened?
In conjuring up supernatural and magical realms, keep the rules of each world simple, clearly defined, and consistent.

Our goal is nothing less than the reinvention of the science fiction television series... [and to jettison] the stock characters, techno-double-talk, bumpy-headed aliens, thespian histrionics, and empty heroics. We want the audience to connect with the characters of Galactica as people. Our characters are not super-heroes. They are not an elite. They are everyday people caught up in an enormous cataclysm and trying to survive it as best they can. They are you and me.

Rather than a strict adherence to the tropes of the sci-fi genre or even the laws of time and space, Moore and his writing staff were cognizant that the audience actually connects to the people – not the mythology and technology.
Establish your series' mythology. All series contain some inherent mythology. At a minimum, this will include the backstories for each main character – which may or may not be completely true.
Whereas central mysteries concern something lost or obscured in the past, and central questions concern the future outcome of problems, a series' mythology concerns the rules of the game.
Create a credible alternative reality.
On a supernatural series, the mythology deals with the rules of magic.

Take dramatic license. Do your research, avoid anachronisms, and aim for verisimilitude. As long as the time period and setting feel right, the audience will allow themselves to get swept away in the dramatic action.
When Setting Up the Rules to an Unfamiliar World, Supernatural or Otherwise, Bring an Outsider Into the World to Help the Audience Get Up to Speed.
If world building is about imagining a different world and constructing its mythology, the converse can also be true: deconstruction.

If families serve as microcosms on virtually all successful episodic TV series, then setting and time period can serve as metaphor for global and universal concerns. 'History doesn't repeat itself, but it often rhymes' (a quote attributed to Mark Twain). On TV, history is more like an echo.
History is a form of storytelling.

Lindelof: The anxiety and the intention going into Watchmen were the same, which was, 'How are we going to live up to this masterwork?' That's certainly what I consider it. There are no words that can describe the impact that Alan Moore and Dave Gibbons's 12 issues had on me.

Lindelof: An immense amount of time and energy went into asking the question, 'What entitles me to get to do this? Why me and why now? Just because it's being offered to me doesn't mean that I'm allowed to do it.' I had to ask myself, 'How do I create a permission structure by which I get to call something Watchmen?'

Lindelof: Watchmen is political and timely. It's not real in the sense of there's a glowing blue guy who has a quantum perception of time, but when you compare it alongside the Marvel characters or, in particular, the DC characters who don't even live in cities that exist in the real world, like Metropolis or Gotham City... Watchmen always took place in our world.
Lindelof: Small details writ large.
Lindelof: So much world building comes from a creator's desire to tell a provocative story with the distance of allegory or science fiction. But Watchmen is allegory that becomes all too real.
Lindelof: The entire process was equal parts audacity and terror every day. There was never a day where I felt like I had control of it.

Lindelof: I was always drawn to comic book storytelling, which is really about the people who are not content to just sit and let the world pass them by. You have to either get out there and change it or escape being eradicated. Those are just the stories that I was drawn to. I don't like stories about people who have given up.

Lindelof: We knew that Dr. Manhattan was hiding in plain sight and that he was probably Calvin 'Cal' Abar. And I say 'probably' because that's the creative space that I am much more comfortable living in. Because the show starts to either accept these ideas or reject them. That's what world building is all about.

Lindelof: It's an exercise in the subconscious and trust. So, when people say, 'Were you making it up as you went along, or was there a very concrete plan?' The answer, at least in terms of my process, is that it gives you space for improvisation that you can't imprint if there's no script. So, you kind of have to have both. It's a process or trial and error, and did I mention trust?
Lindelof: The show starts to inspire the writing process, and that's a feedback loop of which I speak, and you leave space for discovery.

Hillian: In the first season, we focused on the effects one act of violence can have throughout a community. At the center of that story were four men, each representing different stages of manhood. It is really important to Lena that we humanize what so many were just looking at as statistics; each one of these murders or deaths is someone's father, brother, or son; how an act of violence ripples through a community.
Hillian: I know what I don't know. I've done what I can to learn as much as I can about the city, but there's no substitute for hiring writers from Chicago and valuing their expertise.
Hillian: You almost don't even need to balance the tone if you just tell the authentic story of the Black experience in America.
Hillian: I don't think that you have to try to please everyone, but you have to make responsible choices, know exactly why you're making them, and be able to defend them.

Hillian: If I only have one shot, I'm going to make sure I say something. If I go out, I'll go out my way, not trying to make people happy or comfortable, and Lena feels the same way. That's one of the reasons we make a good team.

Hillian: It starts with figuring out what's important to us, what we want to say, and how to say it organically with our characters. Then we look at the season as a whole, map out the beginning, middle, and end almost like three acts: with your first episode serving almost like a pilot, episodes 2, 3, 4 as the first act, episodes 5, 6, 7 is the second act, and 8, 9, 10 is our third act.

Hillian: Yes, it's really important to me – and you taught me thematic unity! I don't necessarily begin every episode with a theme in mind, but once the stories are decided, we do go back and look for ways to meld them with the overarching theme for the season.
Hillian: I just don't think you can do comedy or drama well without the other. For either to be effective, to feel real, you need them both.

McNamara: One of the problems I perceive with historic drama is, as we all know, history is written by the winners. So, there's no 'true story' about it. The other thing is, if you get bogged down in the details and the 'facts,' then you're forced into a structure and you're being forced into making dramatic choices you don't as a dramatist want to make. I don't care about that. What I care about is the essence of this woman.

McNamara: Our writers' room is very steeped in the facts, and yet they know that I'm completely comfortable and even encourage a departure from the facts in favor of character, drama, humor, and irony. But we have the facts at hand so that we're making conscious choices, not mistakes.

McNamara: It really boiled down to her character. It was almost like pitching a contemporary show. I sometimes would pitch the contemporary version of it within the pitch. I'd be like, 'It's like a woman in Chicago wakes up and she realizes she's married the wrong man. He's a fuckwit and she doesn't know whether to kill him or get out of it.' I wanted someone who got it as much as it could be understood if you weren't in my head.

McNamara: I didn't have massive detail, but the pitch was just breaking down how each third of the season would work. I pitched where we thought we'd end up, but I didn't know for sure we'd end up there. I also pitched what the second season would look like. The biggest question they had was about tone.

McNamara: I wrote The Great pilot on spec. I'd written it as a film a few years earlier, but when I met Nick (Nicholas Hoult) on The Favourite, it rekindled my interest in The Great. I wrote the pilot on spec and sent it out unattached to a studio or anyone. We just sent it out to networks and maybe five or six liked it.
McNamara: I think that's why we did a pilot rather than going straight to series. Hulu were, 'Just show us what it is.' I could tell them about the tone, but they needed to see it.

McNamara: That's not really how I write. I'm all about the characters. A little about theme, but not that much. I don't think about metaphor. I really just think about what the character is doing. Theme stuff, all those conversations happen for a few weeks at the start of the writers' room.

McNamara: I have some writers who are contemporary activists on a number of levels, and one of them calls The Great 'post history.' For us, we don't want to see a show that talks about whether being gay is right or wrong. I just want a world in which people are free to have consensual sex with who they want, a world in which people are different races – and no one fucking cares.
McNamara: I'm always interested in what motivates characters to do bad things. It's like watching Walt in Breaking Bad. Is he doing this for his family, or is he just a soulless monster?

McNamara: Two things, I suppose. I only write what I think is true to the characters, and I cast actors who always come from a place of truth, comic truth or dramatic truth. Sometimes, in script, I think I've gone too far joke-wise. That happens less and less as we got the feel of the show. I think it's like M*A*S*H... That is a seminal series because that showed just how farcical and funny you could be if you built the world well enough. And be tragic as well.
McNamara: The world has to be incredibly grounded to get away with parachuting dogs and lemon douches.

A pilot is analogous to a promise you make with the viewer – call it a contract – that tacitly lets them know what to expect if they commit to watching your show every week (or to "binge view"). You're not promising them abject predictability and repetition. You are promising them that you are going to honor what you established in your pilot episode. In TV writing parlance, this is what's known as servicing your franchise.

When a TV executive or producer asks you, "What's the franchise?" of your intended TV series, what they're really asking you is: What are your main characters going to be doing each week? What are the story engines that keep your plotlines moving forward? These engines need fuel, and that fuel is conflict in the form of problems to be solved and internal and external obstacles to be overcome.

In conceiving a TV pilot, your initial creative process might be very similar to writing a screenplay for a feature-length movie: premise, setting, character development – but the tricky part is recognizing that you're not writing toward the ultimate payoff at the climax. Instead, you're getting your audience up to speed on the "arena" (setting, logistics, characters, layers of backstory) of your series – and then setting the stage for a story that unfolds from episode to episode and/or week to week. In this way, the end of your pilot is the beginning of your series.

A central question concerns the future. What's going to happen next? Every episodic TV series needs narrative drive to keep the audience invested. A good central question stokes the audience's curiosity and their need to know more.

We need a clear-cut, central question that demands an answer by the climax of season 1 – and great television series are all about sticking the landing. We continue watching, waiting for answers to the central question and/or central mystery. The central question encompasses the present and future of the characters: what's going to happen to them over the course of the season or series. What's missing is what creates intrigue.
If the storytelling is exceptionally fresh and strong, the audience's expectations will be transcended and their presumptions will always be wrong.
Sustaining our interest in an episodic series is entirely dependent on our investment in the characters. We need to worry about them. We need to make discoveries as they earn them. We need to care.

A central mystery concerns the past. What happened to our characters in the past to make them who they are? How, when, where, and why – and what will be its impact on the present and future of our series regulars?

In trying to determine the specific franchise of your new TV series, focus on the verbs. What are your characters doing each week? They could be investigating, discovering, uncovering, diagnosing, healing, litigating, battling, competing, debating, saving, prosecuting, confronting, framing, stealing, arresting, indicting, avenging, killing, etc. Verbs express characters' episodic goals in the form of micro missions, as the means to an end.

The primary goal for showrunners is to meet – or exceed – their audience's expectations for the types of storylines and basic tone of their given series. New storylines need to be fresh and even break new ground – but within the wheelhouse of that particular show. In other words, a series can break new ground with a new storyline without breaking the entire mold for the show itself.

The most basic type of series franchise is case of the week, which is why there are so many shows about doctors, lawyers, and cops. In a medical drama, these are the medical cases for each new patient. In a law show, these are the legal cases of the plaintiffs and defendants. In a crime show, these are the police and other law enforcement cases.

All procedurals feature mysteries that need to be solved, and the urgency of medical mysteries tends to speed up the pace to relieve the patient's suffering and the audience's despair. Healing, truth, and justice are the lifeblood of all broadcast network procedurals.

In the streaming, on-demand era, slow-burn storytelling is embraced by viewers because it digs deeper into characters, with a greater emphasis on realism or verisimilitude. Instead of case of the week, we now have the season-long procedural in which one case is examined and, ultimately, solved over the course of a full season.

What is justice? – is the central question all procedurals must grapple with, as it depends on race, sexual orientation, gender, socioeconomics, and empathy for the "victims" of a biased and often corrupt system.

Effectively servicing the franchise of a given series puts a new, unpredictable, timely, and provocative spin on a case. Ideally, this new spin will emerge from the characters' relationship to each new case. What psychological and emotional "buttons" might a new case push in a character? What are the main challenges to solving the case? If the case is too easy, it's not viable. It must challenge the series regulars in some way and serve up an inconvenient truth.

Some series, such as HBO's True Detective (especially season 1), are hybrid series – equal parts legal procedural and serialized drama. In True Detective's first season, the mysteries embedded inside its two leads, Rustin Cohle and Martin Hart, and their backstories are equally, if not even more, engrossing than solving the case of the serial killer. This approach enabled True Detective's creator, Nic Pizzolatto, to create his own subgenre: the existential detective story.
In the slower-burn, on-demand era, audiences have a tendency to want to understand not just the legal case, but also its impact on the characters' personal lives, trials, and tribulations.

On streaming platforms, medical procedurals tend to be more hybrid dramas involving physical and/or mental trauma, and the resolution can often be more elusive and morally complex and at times defy simple resolution. These hybrid genre shows tend to be dramas involving hospitalization, diagnosis, therapists, and medical experts; Sharp Objects, Euphoria, The Act, Unbelievable, Ratched, In Treatment, and Legion, to name a few.

The advantage to a franchise that offers closed-ended cases is that viewers can watch each individual episode in any order and still feel satisfied. Serialized shows, on the other hand, require much greater viewer commitment.
In contrast, serialized single-camera comedies and dramedies (either half-hour or one-hour) contain incremental character evolution from episode to episode and from season to season.

In the limited anthology series, each season is encompassed under the umbrella of a theme or within a frame of one long-form story per season, usually featuring a whole new cast or a repertory company of actors playing completely different roles each season.
It's an easy question to answer, but much harder to pull off when the story engines are all about the relationships and family dynamics.

In a softer concept series, the basic franchise is the exploration of the main characters' quest for the ecstasy of success versus the agony of defeat – and coping with the interstices. A useful way to articulate the franchise of a "soft" concept show is, "In each episode, the characters will struggle to achieve ______." The specific struggles of the characters are the source of both drama and comedy.

In determining the franchise for your series, you might conceive both the overarching, central question for the long haul, as well as the more finite season arcs – also in the form of questions – from season to season. Where and when do you hope to land the plane?

When "breaking story," think of the plotting of A, B, C stories as micro missions. In procedural dramas, the micro missions are the proactive steps taken to solve the case of the week. In non-procedurals, the micro missions are more specific and organically emerge from character to character.

Servicing the franchise of a series is not only about choosing which cases and/or plotlines to explore, but also about the pace of delivering new information, clues, discoveries, and resolutions. A big part of servicing a franchise is hitting the "sweet spot" of your series: the intersection of character development and franchise, rooted in the overall tone and overarching theme(s) of the show.
The best endings are unpredictable, but inevitable.

In some series, these questions are thematic. In Ozark, Barry, Euphoria, and Unorthodox, the overarching theme is: Can you ever escape your past [transgressions]? In Pose: Will trans people ever find mainstream acceptance? In Schitt's Creek: Does money buy happiness? In Westworld: What does it mean to be human?

To sustain an episodic series, each crisis will ideally impact most or all of the show's other characters with ripple effects; I refer to these choices as story tentacles. When "breaking story," choose the plotline that leads to more story. Ask yourself: How many other characters' lives can be organically and credibly impacted by one story choice? Seek fertile ground versus more finite one-offs (aka dead ends).

Characters need proactive, positive goals while avoiding potential negative consequences. Without this positive/negative charge, there is little dramatic tension, and without tension, there is no drama or comedy.

The difference between a mediocre series and a great one is that the latter's showrunners are always digging for a new vein of gold. Depending upon the genre of the series, the gold within each episode will be the moral dilemmas and gray areas of the main character(s). Mining the layers of a story is key, along with keeping the stakes as high as possible. All successful TV series are about winning and losing.

Central questions and mysteries stoke our curiosity. We're waiting to see how a crime/legal/medical/love story is going to play out. As long as we keep wondering and anticipating and discussing and posting – we're going to keep watching. As soon as all questions are answered, the series must either introduce new central questions or end.

The difficulty is the show was never really that much about the client. When you see a lot of network shows, you can feel the network notes: "I have to care about the stakes in this," which always means dramatizing the client. But we have a limited number of pages. We do 58-page scripts for a 42½-minute show. For us, the stakes were always, for example, will Alicia win because if she loses, her job will be in danger.

On The Good Fight, our main cast is a character. That's why we have the most fun when the show starts turning internal. We did an episode on The Good Wife called "Red Team, Blue Team" which was a mock court between our associates and our partners. In that episode, suddenly there was civil war within the firm. And that was so exciting because then you didn't have these straw men on the other side. That episode really engaged the audience to choose sides with their favorite characters within the firm.
I would say 98 percent of TV is setting up straw men and straw women to be knocked down because everybody would rather enjoy propaganda than drama. Propaganda is confirming what you already believe.
Theoretically, the characters that we like are our regular cast members. The goal is to have them disagreeing with one another, and being able to recognize that each one has a valid point.

What we're usually doing is throwing two concepts together that don't necessarily seem to fit and then try to find a way as you weave your way through it. Our characters multitask, so they're learning something from one case that influences the other.
So I think you don't build with theme in mind, but you can't help but write with theme in mind. We just don't call it theme; we call making a deadline.

If for some reason we were building it for the network, I think the pushback, if there were any, would come from the structural oddities of how we chose to tell these stories, as opposed to the politics of the show; I think that would be more of an issue at a network; they tend to want things more clearly tied up at the end. Ambiguity is not anybody's friend on network television.

For anybody who was brought up on either LA Law, Law & Order, The Practice, any of those series, there had to be some dramatic stakes. It's like if you played tennis without a net; everybody would just be crazy. This view of how a courtroom works certainly goes out the window if you give a certain number of people these "Get Out of Jail Free" cards, because then the courtroom is kind of a joke. And only people that are considered "bad" end up there.
The most essential ingredient to great storytelling is empathy. How does a writer foster empathy? Through character vulnerability.

For viewers to develop a relationship with a character over many episodes, there must be such a level of complexity in their character that whatever internal problems they have can be solved only through external interactions, with other characters.

Adding to the complexity is the fact that what characters need the most is paradoxically often what they resist the most. As you draft your pilot, what's the first impression your characters make? It's not an absolute, but our first impressions are almost always wrong. And we want our viewers to be wrong, because if what we see is what we get, there is no complexity, mystery, or anything to discover about the character as the series progresses.

All heroes are depicted as having an expertise with a liability. When I interviewed Breaking Bad's creator/showrunner Vince Gilligan, he espoused that he constructs characters by giving them a "grounded superpower." In the case of Walt, it was his "ability for self-delusion."

Even superheroes have vulnerabilities. This positive/negative charge is how energy works; it's also the way drama (and comedy) works. Characters' internal relationships to their specific strengths and corresponding weaknesses is what makes a somewhat generic character into an iconic one.

As we develop our characters, we often find the most difficult challenge for them to face is in the situations where they need to forgive, forget, and move on. Characters who tend to be the most iconic and indelible are stubborn, more likely to resist change and make pronouncements that they are never going to forgive or forget something. It's irrevocable, irreparable, and seemingly impossible to forgive a particularly heinous transgression or breach of trust – the lifeblood of good drama.

Hypothesis. Set up a character's positive attributes and corresponding flaws. This happens via the writer assigning a cluster of distinctive traits to each character, and then adding emotion. As the story progresses, the circumstances of the story will cause the characters – and us – to evaluate their actions.

Alignment. Does the story align us with why the characters are the way they are – their goals, motives, and psychology? How readily do we align with these goals and beliefs? Allegiance. Do we "agree" with the character, i.e., start to feel a sense of identification or loyalty towards them? Do we empathize and/or feel sympathy for their values?

This hypothesis/alignment/allegiance process is at the core of empathy and sympathy for storytellers. Not only must we emotionally track characters from scene to scene, episode to episode, and season to season, but we must also ask ourselves how we want the audience to feel. In this way, all effective storytelling generates an active conversation or debate between the characters' actions and the audience's emotional response (laughter, tears, outrage, fear) to those actions. Ultimately, this is what Aristotle called catharsis.

Empathy is dependent on point of view. It's circumstantial, and Succession achieves this brilliantly. Pride and hubris are vices of the ego, and every member of the Roy clan is corrupted, which places this satirical, funny series in the genre of family noir.

For me, that becomes the central question on Succession. Instead of rooting for the Ewing/Carrington families to stay together, we're wondering why the Roys don't fall apart. Succession is a series that dares us to care about its main characters, a series in which only the hired help, staff, and outsiders (Tom, Greg, Willa, Tabitha, Naomi, Jennifer) trying to gain entry into the Roy clan are wholly sympathetic.

Showrunner Armstrong's worldview (as expressed in Succession) isn't about nihilism or idealism. It's about the price of greed, and its ripple effects in a world in which, as Logan is apt to say, "Money wins."

A prestige TV series like Succession could not exist on a broadcast television network – today or ever. The remarkably prolific late TV mogul Aaron Spelling (who executive-produced Dynasty) was notorious for not showing the ugly underbelly behind the façade of glamour.
Like so many of the exceptional TV series explored in this book, WandaVision is a show about loss, and the ultimate superpower: forgiveness.

When I wrote it, the honest truth is I had no regard for thinking about who would be likable, who you would root for. I suppose your empathy does need to engage at some level, probably. But it's not the way I think about these things. Maybe because I come from comedy and the way that you engage with a comic figure is possibly a different way from how the audience is normally invited to engage in a drama.

I've got a bunch of different ways of thinking about the empathy question. One is that, as a writer, you need to have the ability to imagine yourself into other people's shoes, and I tend to think that the viewers will share that. That while they may not be thinking that these are wonderful people they're observing, they might nevertheless be able to imagine what it could be like to be them.

I think my final feeling on the empathy question is that it might not be that important whether you can root for the characters or like them so long as you know that the show has an attitude towards them. If there is that sense invisibly pervading the show, then I think it's bearable.

But the tone is a delicate balancing act. If the level of satirical judgment or dramatic irony is too much, and you can feel the hand of the author too heavily, then the show falls flat, the tension is gone. Too much authorial direction, instruction, and it's all dead. Too little and I'd say it was sort of pointless, or flabby, you lose the rationale for putting anything in or taking anything out.

Often the family dynamic, the mom or dad are so fascinating, beguiling, powerful, that nothing in the world feels quite as alive or thrilling. It's impossible to leave alone. Like a drug or a screwed-up but enthralling relationship. Something you can't stop returning to. And I think that's a tragic situation. Right? The tragedy of these people to some extent is that they can never quite find anything as interesting anywhere in the world as their family.

The Roys also have a codependent relationship to power; it's like an addiction. Each child has an attachment to their damaged pasts without going to therapy and trying to heal. What I extract is, it's their self-loathing as punishment for white privilege. They're all haunted by shame, but it's repressed and camouflaged by the material trappings of wealth.

I would hope we've got sufficiently complicated characters that you believe them when sometimes they behave in awful ways and sometimes good ways. They're consistently inconsistent, like most human beings.

The best bits of the writers' room are a bunch of observations like that that become contentions and turn into arguments about character and what's immovable and constant about character, and what is the margin for change, growth, or diminishment.

With Logan, his media empire (specifically newspapers) is shifting in the wake of technology. He's outdated, and his health is betraying him, too. He's weak and aged. I think that's the main reason we empathize with him from the very beginning. Mortality and illness are life's great equalizers.

The structure of the room is to spend a month or more on broad themes, character shapes, stuff, jokes, material for moments that might happen. But the main thing I'm hoping for is to feel the shape of the seasons. Things change a lot. You know, you find brilliant performance, and you want to write more for a certain actor; or locations change, and you have to cut pages and shift story. But essentially, we know the story that we're headed. When we were writing the first episode, we knew what was going to happen in the final episode of the season.

And I like that a lot. I know that some people can feel constrained or that it feels too rigid. But, for me, it gives me a greater sense of confidence, offers more freedom in that we can smell the plot and character roses along the way. I would be not very relaxed if I didn't know where we were heading.

Part of that's what you're talking about, I love, the details. I feel like one of the things you do when you first appraise a show – or a person, or a film, but in this case the show – is you decide how much you trust your whole endeavor. And I think it's really important to get the texture right, that the graphics on the news look like real graphics and the locations feel authentic.

The concentric circles of who knows what about. A scandal is tough sometimes to keep hold of. And you just have to research it well and keep all that information in your mind. There are also concentric circles of certainty, I think, for a showrunner and a show as an entity.

The more things you have in orbit that are a part of your emotional solar system, the more gravity you have going on you. Knowing the family history, I think, provides a sense of solidity to the audience about the world that they're inhabiting.

Every television series is about a family – whether they're related by blood or not. Some series are literally about a family: Succession, Schitt's Creek, Black-ish, The Chi, Ozark, Modern Family, This Is Us, The Crown, and, of course, Game of Thrones. Some focus on a workplace "family": The Office, Black Monday, Ted Lasso, Brooklyn Nine-Nine, Grey's Anatomy, The Good Doctor, The Good Fight; while others focus on a family of friends: Friends, I May Destroy You, The Good Place, Insecure, Community.

The goal for every new TV series is for it to feel fresh in its own right – which means that all TV families need to be imperfect and, on some level, discontented. Or, as Leo Tolstoy wrote in the first sentence of Anna Karenina: "Happy families are all alike; every unhappy family is unhappy in its own way."

Today's audiences may not be able to relate to what it's like being a KGB spy in the 1980s, a money-laundering puppet for a cartel, a royal, or a football coach. But we can all relate to what it feels like to be in a family, which is the main point of this chapter: Family stories are universal.

Some series focus on workplace colleagues that interact as a family unit might (Grey's Anatomy, Scandal, Le Bureau des Légendes, The Wire), encompassing the dynamics of authority figures (mom and dad), siblings (rivalries and bonding), spouses, wise elders (grandparents), prodigal sons and daughters, black-sheep outcasts, and some characters that act like children: from tantrum-prone toddlers to rebellious teenagers.
You choose your friends, but you can't choose your family.

In Succession, aging billionaire/patriarch/tycoon Logan Roy (Brian Cox) wields his power over his adult children like an emotional cudgel by withholding his approval and validation from his heir(s) apparent. Logan represents the antithesis of unconditional love. His relationships with his kids are all transactional.

All "family" members, including friends and colleagues, can be adept at pushing each other's psychological buttons (aka emotional triggers). The better someone knows you, the more familiar they are with your weaknesses and vulnerabilities. In other words, the people who love you most know how to hurt you the most. Trust in close relationships is built out of respect for these boundaries.
Familial power dynamics influence the choices characters make in order to feel loved, reassured, respected, and validated from season to season.

Family dynamics are especially useful when assembling an ensemble cast. It's not that you'll need to cover every gender, age, or familial role. But it is helpful to determine how each character might relate to one another in both positive and negative ways.

All relationships are like mirrors, and each person we encounter reflects our sense of self right back to us. They can also remind us of someone else either in appearance, attitude, or how they make us feel. This causes characters to project their past onto new individuals – perhaps overreacting to a quasi-judgmental remark that (usually subconsciously) reminds them of a judgmental parent, former lover, or estranged family member.
The subtext of virtually any scene is familial power dynamics, psychological wounds from the past, and subtle movements that reaffirm clan position.

Great series are about great relationships that are consciously and/or unconsciously based on family dynamics. Where does your series "live"? What's its primary arena? What's its tonal "sweet spot"? As you ponder these questions, bear in mind that home is where the heart is.

The show is so much about emotions and character . . . it's not an issue-based show. It's funny because the assignment was issue-based, but I'm like, if I just embody gentrification in the two sisters, I never have to worry about it. I never have to be didactic or anything. The characters just embody the issue.
There's not one sex scene on Vida that is gratuitous. It always either shifts the character or reaffirms a narrative. So that's super important, especially when you're dealing with queer sex scenes.
Our families have the potential to love us the most, but also to hurt us the most. It's complicated, and you don't try to manufacture a "happy ending."

The basis of our show comes from family experience; [it is] for sure also about the danger even more than about love. Yes, it is about the search for love, and looking for the approval of my mother and father. But, at the same time, they push me somewhere that maybe I don't want to go. I'm not ready to go. It's not my place to go. So it's really the center of the show.

Yes, your family is your support, but, at the same time, it's something that you have to free yourself from in order to find yourself. There is a funny contradiction in that the family itself is a value in the organized crime world.

When you betray your family or you betray your group, you are part of the world that accepts you. It feels very painful to say, "I don't want to be like you anymore," or even, "I don't agree with you." It looks like a big betrayal. This culture is very strong.

We have to deal with reality. We cannot just invent things, because it's not correct with this kind of story, people die. One of our consultants brought us in for a private talk. We met in a cemetery, and while we were talking about all his life, he was walking around the graves, saying, "This was my friend; he died at age 22. And this one died at 27. This one was 31." Like a war. This told us we had to be realistic about the death toll.

The decision was to create the perception that you are completely in the eyes of the character. Each episode is in the POV of one specific character. And being and empathizing totally with this character, you realize you don't know when you're going to die. You can be the best actor in the world, but if you have read all the scripts and you know that at the end you're going to die, it can influence how you play the role in each "present" moment of the story. So, we don't tell them.

Streaming programs that drop us into the lives of characters we can relate to, whose problems resonate with us, satisfies much more than a hunger for entertainment, escapism, or distraction. Television today provides audiences with empathy on demand.

The best television writing has an external dimension. In other words, we have to know more than just our characters' plot-level wants and needs in the series, the season, the story, the subplot, and the scenes. We're also compelled to understand who they are, what they fear, what they hope for – the subtext, the emotional challenge, the dimension of their internal struggles. We need to grasp the why.

Great storytelling demonstrates that vulnerability is not a weakness, but a strength. Of course, being vulnerable requires the courage to be our most authentic selves in the present moment, despite our past traumas – which cause emotional voids, our coping mechanisms to avoid vulnerability.

In real life, people will do just about anything to avoid facing difficult emotions and uncomfortable conversations. But in our second lives, we're compelled by discomfort; it draws us in. That's what good drama and comedy are all about: we can vicariously experience catharsis.
Shame is universal.

Generational trauma is at the center of everything in Mare of Easttown – including, as it turns out, the central murder mystery that propels the show forward – but for no one is that truer than for Mare Sheehan.
Mare of Easttown is that rarest of mysteries – structurally solid as a piece of genre-writing, yet infinitely more fascinating as a character study that happens to involve a murder.
In this town, everyone is atoning for something.

Ingelsby's Easttown, a fictional township in Delaware County, Pennsylvania, is so specifically drawn that it takes on an air of universality. We all know someone who lives in an Easttown. These traumas, shames, and emotional voids are familiar if not personally relatable.

The critique is the fabric of the show itself. By presenting a very specific reality, we are shown what is unacceptable. Inadequate mental health care cost Mare a father and a son. Rampant opioid abuse creates addicts – some that see no option but to steal or sell their bodies.

I May Destroy You is a journey through the stages of trauma and personal grief. Sadly, the mystery that drives the story – what happened on the night in question – isn't particularly groundbreaking. But the way in which that storytelling device is used to mine the soul of the show's protagonist feels totally revolutionary.

Throughout the series, Coel presents us with conflict after conflict where both or all parties are, on some level, operating in the right but where a lack of self-knowledge and the presence of unaddressed pain prevents these competing egos from communicating effectively with one another.
The person she needs to forgive isn't some abuser but herself. In doing so, she's released from the shame and despair that's haunted her since the story began.

Coming-of-age stories, particularly ones concerning young love, are often wistful or told with an undercurrent of nostalgia for better, simpler times. It's the nostalgia of those who selectively or willfully misremember their youths told in a manner that soothes or comforts their aging minds. Sally Rooney and Alice Birch's Normal People takes a battering ram to that form of nostalgia and presents an unflinching look at the complexity and emotional vulnerability of experiencing everything for the first time.

I believe identifying shame is vital to character development, especially the way 'shame' is defined by Dr. Brené Brown, author of the bestselling book Daring Greatly, as the fear of disconnection. Dr. Brown is referring to disconnection from the community. Shame rears its ugly head when we believe there's something wrong with us, that we are, in some fundamental way, deficient.

Deep down, we don't feel loveable or good enough, and so we try to conform to what we presume other people want us to be – and hide those parts of ourselves that we're convinced are unacceptable. It's both an inner renunciation of Self and something we project outward.

At the age of 19, Esty already feels inadequate as daughter, granddaughter, and wife, so the prospect of becoming a mother terrifies her and motivates her exodus from the Williamsburg shtetl. For me, all of Esty's initial actions are motivated by shame.

There are many ways to tell a story in episodic television, and each approach differs depending on your series' premise and point of view (POV). So, let's start with this basic question: Whose story is it?
Primarily utilized to show the world of the series through one dominant perspective, this limited POV is particularly important because often the series' lead has something to hide.

In each of the preceding examples, the audience is privy to the protagonist's vulnerabilities, whereas all or many of the other characters are (mostly) presented with a façade or persona of the protagonist rather than someone willing to disclose their secrets and ulterior motives.
Sherlock needs Watson, but he's loath to admit it, which fuels their bromance with humor and conflict. Walter White and Jesse Pinkman have the same dynamic on Breaking Bad.

In Better Call Saul, protagonist Jimmy McGill incrementally and inevitably invents his alias, Saul Goodman, but his transformation is heavily influenced by his business partner/partner in crime: his girlfriend, Kim Wexler. Kim is the pivotal character (aka agent of change) in the series' latter seasons, and the cliffhanger at the conclusion of season 5 shocks and surprises us as much as it does Jimmy/Saul when Kim proposes marriage.

What differentiates these series from being pure 'ensemble' dramedies is the dominance of their lead characters' POV. Sex and the City begins and ends with Carrie's newspaper column, told via V.O. and setting up each episode's main theme.

Voice-Over (V.O.) Narration as Running Commentary on Current Action. This is generally used to widen perspective and place events in a thematic or ironic context. Use sparingly, and never to narrate something we're seeing for ourselves, but rather as counterpoint, irony, and subtext. Beware of U.V.O.: Unnecessary Voice-Over.

It's the articulation of his inner psychology in voice-over that enables us to grasp at the why. The V.O. keeps the audience inside Dexter's head and tries to help us reconcile his violent actions. His emotionless voice lulls us and draws us in. And, even though his killing sprees compel us to turn away, we keep watching again and again – because while Dexter is a monster, he's a well-intentioned monster.

Dual protagonist POVs are almost always improved and enhanced when each of the equal protagonists have different opinions, values, and perspectives, as well as their symbiotic, complementary strengths and weaknesses. The basic idea behind this theory is that if you put two incomplete people together, you get one whole, well-rounded individual.
In ensemble shows, several characters might be involved in the same A, B, or even C story. There can be a lot of crossover.
The main purpose of flashbacks is to inform present-day dilemmas or provide the key to solving a central mystery. They can also be used to provide subtext.

In The Queen's Gambit, flashbacks are employed to illuminate Beth Harmon's backstory, particularly her formative years spent in an orphanage – giving foundational character insight to her present-day behavior and outlook on the world.
Once you establish the dominant POV(s), the next challenge for writers is how to externalize inner psychology through action, subtext, and narrative 'devices.'
Watchmen, which is set 34 years after the events in the comic books, makes jumps in time not only on a series level, but also within episodes.

Subtext is unspoken communication conveyed through intonation, facial expressions, and body language. As we all know, what people say and what they mean are often diametrically opposed. As Aristotle espoused over 2,000 years ago: characters are defined by their actions, not by what they say, but rather by what they do – and why.

Why is it so hard for human beings to speak the truth? Shame. Fear of disconnection and/or rejection. In other cases, we're afraid of hurting someone else's feelings. More often, it seems easier to soften the truth – which often compounds and makes everything worse.
Information is power. It's not just about who does what, when, but who knows what, when.

The audience is innocent, but not naïve.

POV impacts dialogue by projecting the past on the present moment.

Subtext gives voice to what is not spoken but implied. Subtext is primarily articulated through body language, facial expressions, intonation and/or awkward pauses. No matter how brilliantly you can write dialogue, do not overlook the profound value of silence.

Antagonists can take many forms – and are not always personified.

In Chernobyl, the antagonist is the Soviet power structure. In Barry, the main antagonist is PTSD from Barry's traumatic past as a Marine deployed to Afghanistan. In Mare of Easttown and Fleabag, the main antagonist is grief. In Hacks, the main antagonist is the fear of obscurity. In Succession, the main antagonist is greed.
All villains are antagonists, but not all antagonists are villains. In many romantic comedies, the love interests are each other's antagonists.

The antagonist will be the primary (usually negative) person or force that most actively challenges or threatens your protagonist – thereby directly impeding your protagonist from attaining their main goals.

Antagonists tend to be parasites who outwardly seek to get power from others – by any means necessary: deception, theft, murder, manipulation – as opposed to protagonists, who tend to discover that their power lies within.
Antagonists almost always try to subvert the truth. Usually, their intention is to stay in control and/or to maintain the status quo, even if they're miserable.

Secrets and lies are the currency in Big Little Lies, Euphoria, Your Honor, Better Call Saul, Ted Lasso, The Undoing, Tehran, Lupin, Fargo, Bridgerton, Babylon Berlin, The Marvelous Mrs. Maisel, Younger, Shtisel, Dead to Me, Barry, Underground Railroad, The Sinner, Insecure, and so many more engrossing shows.

Antagonists are roadblocks or speed bumps in a protagonist's micro missions. Episodic series encompass overarching goals for each main character in a season or over the course of the whole series. But, as we all know, as soon as that primary goal is successfully attained, the series is usually over – unless it pivots to another overarching mission or goal.

Most villains do not change. While their specific goals may change, antagonists are the same at the end of the story as they were at the beginning. True villains do not evolve or learn from their failures. If the villain were to evolve, to grow, then they would no longer be the villain.
The antagonist will often have the same goal as the protagonist – but with a negative or evil agenda.
Your enemy's enemy is your friend.
If your plot is lacking dramatic tension/conflict, you'll need to make your antagonist stronger.
In many cases, the true antagonist is not revealed as the enemy until the climax.
The most iconic antagonists possess both positive and negative characteristics – and are uniquely flawed in both sympathetic and nefarious ways.
Of course, some memorable antagonists are just pure evil. Every villain is the hero of his/her/their own story.
While it's generally best to personify and personalize an antagonist, sometimes the source of antagonism is systemic, all-encompassing, and/or oblique.

Man against society: Characters often have the burden of facing a social construct far beyond their control – be it the Regency era ruling structure in England on Bridgerton, the monarchy in The Crown and The Great, or the cops in The Wire, in which the unstoppable drug trade corrupts the city of Baltimore.
Man against self: Sometimes the main character is his own worst enemy. In Mad Men, Don Draper fights to conceal his checkered past, a far cry from his urbane advertising executive persona.

Man against multiple forces: As evidenced by some of the preceding examples, antagonistic forces can intersect. The more obstacles, the better, so long as they're organic to the story. Intersecting antagonistic forces are most effective when they're thematically linked.

Theme is a central idea expressed through action. It can add dimension and resonance. In episodic television, theme is the glue that holds multiple, and sometimes divergent, storylines together. Theme is what the story is really about, more than just a series of events, but some kind of underlying universal truth about life.
On a TV series, theme can be articulated by a character – through dialogue or V.O. or some other narrative device. But, for me, theme works best as subtext – on a subliminal level – for the audience.
