A cliffhanger is a plot device that leaves one or more characters facing either a dangerous situation or a shocking revelation. As all drama is essentially manipulation (and not reality), a cliffhanger leaves today's spoiler-sensitive streaming audiences with a tantalizing paradox: 'I can't wait to find out what happens next – don't tell me.'

Today, forcing a cliffhanger at the end of each act break is deadly. And 'writing to the act break' via contrivance no longer applies. Instead, act breaks can land on a turning point that emerges from characters, not merely from plot.

Suspense is key to all satisfying cliffhangers in all genres. Suspense has two components: Anticipation (of what's going to happen next) and Surprise (when it actually happens, hopefully in an unexpected, inevitable but not predictable way).

Cliffhangers often hinge on survival (literal or psychological/emotional). Without risk and something valuable at stake, there is no drama or comedy, and without the upside of reward or potential reward, there is no drama or comedy. You'll need the possibility of the positive and negative to generate suspense.

Seek out the positive (+) and negative (−) charge in each episode, scene, act, and make sure you have sufficient dramatic tension. The polarity of a +/− charge is how energy works. It's also the lifeblood of satisfying drama and comedy.

We would go episode by episode, breaking it all down: A, B, and C. It was always in A, B, and C structure. We really used those table reads not as a gauge for whether the episode is funny, because we knew that when it got on its feet on set, it would be, but rather as a structural thing. For the writers, we were in there not thinking about whether a joke landed, but rather, 'Does the structure of this episode work? Do the stories make sense?'

The mirror has always been about people's self-reflection. It came about because, for me, being in the bathroom was the only place where I'm facing the rawest version of myself. And I'm cut off from the outside world.

In writing, I obviously need to laugh and find it funny, that the moment of truth, and to hear it out loud amongst the writers in the room is probably the most helpful thing we do. We kind of get into character and then can see what plays.
Pitch docs, series bibles, and treatments are used exclusively as selling tools (again, proof-of-concept) rather than as roadmaps that must be strictly followed in the writers' room.
As the adage goes: you don't get a second chance to make a good first impression.

Every pitch needs to be customized based on the project and the potential buyers. To use baking a cake as metaphor, every pitch needs to contain all or most of the following ingredients, but not necessarily in this order.
Why this idea? How did you come up with it? What was the catalyst? How is it wholly original, or a fresh spin or take on a familiar idea? Why do you feel it will connect with an audience?

Why you as its author/creator/producer? I have found it's a good icebreaker to begin a pitch with your personal passion and connection to this story and these characters. Why are you uniquely qualified and compelled to write this pilot and/or to steer this series?

Why now? Timing is everything. Look at the world around you, near and far. What outrages you? What stories are not being told or told well? How can your project fill a void of stories set in a particular world or about a unique group of people?
The more specific you make something, the more universal it becomes.

Your pitch presentation should have the same tone as the genre of the series. If it's a comedy, your pitch had better be funny. If it's an edgy thriller, it needs to offer suspense, thrills, and chills. If it's a family drama, the pitch needs to evoke empathy and pathos.

Once you've conveyed your personal passion and set the stage, it's time to tell them the story. Where to begin? Pitch out the teaser or cold open succinctly, but with just the right amount of detail, to establish tone and hook them in.

Pitch out the pilot episode, starting with a desperate protagonist in need. Urgency helps. Less is more. Choose your details wisely. Verbs are your friends as they convey proactive goals and stakes (or consequences).

Clarify the mythology/world building in your series. Briefly and succinctly pitch out the basic mythology and/or origin story of the series. Your series' mythology will take the form of a central mystery – secrets from the past that are actively hidden or obscured.

Remember, there is no need to tell the execs everything you know. Not knowing what's going to happen invokes the central questions of your series to sustain your series over time. An effective pitch will entice your listeners to ask you questions.

Fuel your story engines to illustrate the sustainability of your series after the pilot. This is your series' franchise. Have at least three examples for future episodes. What are the 'story engines,' i.e., what will drive the series from episode to episode?

Stick the landing: End your pitch of the pilot episode on a high note that portends future conflicts versus too much resolution and harmony. The primary difference between a TV series and a movie is, by and large, a movie is intended to be finite. In contrast, the end of a TV pilot is just the beginning of what's going to be an ongoing journey for the characters.

Know your genre inside and out. If your ambition is to create a new, groundbreaking sci-fi series, do your homework and know about every sci-fi series past, present, and on the horizon. Remember, you're not just competing with what's currently on the air; you're also competing with every series in the same genre from the past decade and more.

Show and tell them your series is set on fertile ground. How will it connect to an audience and captivate them over time? How will your series last beyond season 1, assuming it's not a limited series?

'Sit back in your chair.' This is simplest and best advice I've ever been given about how to comport oneself in a professional meeting. When we appear prepared, engaged, and confident (but not arrogant), we allow the potential buyers to relax and enjoy our presentation.

Make them care. Empathy, empathy, empathy. Get them invested in the plight of your characters. Keep them on the edge of their seats with suspense – which is generated by their emotional investment in the characters.

Finish the pitch and don't buy it back. What this means is when you're done with your rehearsed pitch: shut up. This is crucial to (potentially) closing the sale. Do not give in to this temptation. When you're done pitching, try to embrace the silence with confidence.

Don't burn any bridges. When you go into a network or studio executive's office, it's extremely important to remember that your #1 priority is not to make the sale. Your #1 priority is to establish rapport with the exec.
Be overprepared, just in case. I generally recommend that you have three pitches prepared when you're meeting with a producer. If you strike out with one, move on to the next one.

We pitched verbally. Then we sent in written documents: the pitch document that is the concept and main narrative arcs of the series as well as the summary of the pitch conversation and at the same time we send the 'DEV PRO' (Development Process) document that outlines to the broadcaster what we're going to do, how we're going to do it, who we're going to do it with, how much it's going to cost and what rights are included.

We own 100 percent of the asset. We control the brand. We control the trademark. And we own 100 percent of the asset. We only license the asset around the world; we do not sell it for more than a license period.

One of our principles of writing, one of our rules, was you don't keep any good ideas for the next season. In France, we say, 'You burn all your ships.' All the ideas you have, you put even in the season you are writing now. Don't keep anything for the next one.

All the characters have to be a mystery because, if not, if you know them too quickly, you can't do a show with them because the show is for five years. You can't get tired of them. And to do that, we have to keep some piece of mystery. It's very interesting to discover new angles and new faces of the characters that you can't do initially.
The best pitches are the ones where they go, 'We'd like to read your treatment, your pitch document.' And then they read and say, 'Now we want to talk, start the conversation.'

A series is not just a deal; it's an emotionally engaged product. People are going to give you 10 to 24 hours a season to look at something. That is an enormous amount of time, and, therefore, you need to respect them if you want to get that respect back.
The creators most passionate about their shows are the ones that usually move ahead. That's the truth.
